qa_pairs.question: How can Discrete Phase Model (DPM) injections be automated in Ansys Fluent using journal file commands, and what is the typical format for a steady injection text file?
qa_pairs.answer: To automate DPM injections in Ansys Fluent, journal file commands can be added to your journal file after solution initialization. For example, you can use `/define/models/dpm/interaction/coupled-calculations yes` to enable interaction with the continuous phase, and `/define/models/dpm/injections/create injection-0:1 no yes file no zinjection01.inj no no no no` to create an injection from a file. A basic manually created steady injection text file, like `zinjection01.inj`, generally includes a header for `z` values, followed by a line defining particle properties such as `x`, `y`, `z` coordinates, `u`, `v`, `w` velocities, `diameter`, `temperature`, `mass-flow`, `mass`, `frequency`, `time`, and a `name` for the injection, all enclosed in parentheses. The precise format is detailed in the `2024R2 Fluent Customization Manual` under the 'Steady File Format' subsection.

qa_pairs.question: How do users submit Ansys CFX jobs for parallel computation on Alliance clusters using Slurm scripts, and what are the primary command-line options available for the `cfx5solve` solver?
qa_pairs.answer: Ansys CFX jobs can be submitted to Alliance clusters for parallel computation using Slurm scripts. For single-node parallel execution, the `cfx5solve` command typically uses `-start-method "Open MPI Local Parallel"` (for `narval` or `fir` clusters) or `"Intel MPI Local Parallel"` (for other clusters), along with `-part $SLURM_CPUS_ON_NODE` to specify the number of cores. For multi-node distributed parallel execution, the command uses `-start-method "Open MPI Distributed Parallel"` or `"Intel MPI Distributed Parallel"` and `-par-dist $NNODES`, where `NNODES` is determined using `slurm_hl2hl.py`. Key command-line options for `cfx5solve` include `-def YOURFILE.def` to specify the definition file, `-single` for single precision (the default), `-double` for double precision (which doubles memory requirements), and `-large` to support meshes with up to 2 billion elements. A comprehensive list of command-line options can be viewed by running `cfx5solve -help` after loading the relevant Ansys module.
